questions.question: Derive the mirror formula for a spherical mirror using ray diagrams and geometrical relations.
questions.answer: Using a concave mirror ray diagram, consider a ray parallel to the principal axis reflecting through the focus and another passing through the centre of curvature. Using similar triangles formed by object, image and focus points, the relation between object distance (u), image distance (v) and focal length (f) is obtained as 1/f = 1/v + 1/u.

questions.question: Derive the expression for linear magnification produced by a spherical mirror and relate it with object and image distances.
questions.answer: Magnification (m) is defined as the ratio of image height to object height. Using similar triangles in the mirror diagram, m = h'/h = -v/u. The negative sign indicates that real images formed by mirrors are inverted.

questions.question: An object is placed 15 cm in front of a concave mirror of focal length 10 cm. Calculate the position, size and nature of the image if the object height is 3 cm.
questions.answer: Using mirror formula: 1/f = 1/v + 1/u. Here u = -15 cm, f = -10 cm. 1/v = -1/10 + 1/15 = -1/30, so v = -30 cm. Magnification m = -v/u = -(-30)/(-15) = -2. Image height = m × object height = -2 × 3 = -6 cm. The image is real, inverted and enlarged.

questions.question: Explain the formation of images by a concave mirror for all possible object positions with ray diagrams.
questions.answer: For object at infinity → image at focus (real, inverted, highly diminished). Beyond C → image between C and F (real, inverted, diminished). At C → image at C (same size). Between C and F → image beyond C (enlarged). At F → image at infinity. Between F and P → image behind mirror (virtual, erect, enlarged).

questions.question: Explain image formation by convex mirrors and discuss why they are useful in security and traffic systems.
questions.answer: Convex mirrors diverge parallel rays and form virtual, erect and diminished images behind the mirror. Because they provide a wide field of view, they are used in rear-view mirrors, security mirrors in shops and at road intersections.

questions.question: Derive Snell's law of refraction using the wave theory concept of change in speed of light between two media.
questions.answer: When light passes from one medium to another its speed changes. The ratio of sine of the angle of incidence to sine of the angle of refraction remains constant for a given pair of media. Thus Snell's law: sin i / sin r = constant = refractive index.
answer_outline: Final relation sin i / sin r

questions.question: Derive the relation between apparent depth and real depth when an object is viewed from air above water.
questions.answer: Due to refraction at the water-air interface, light rays bend away from the normal. Using Snell's law and small-angle approximation, the relation is obtained as Apparent depth = Real depth / refractive index.

questions.question: Explain the phenomenon of total internal reflection and derive the formula for critical angle.
questions.answer: Total internal reflection occurs when light travels from a denser to a rarer medium and the angle of incidence exceeds the critical angle. At critical angle, angle of refraction is 90°. Using Snell's law: sin C = 1/μ.

questions.question: Explain how optical fibres transmit light signals using total internal reflection.
questions.answer: Optical fibres consist of a core and cladding with different refractive indices. Light entering the core undergoes repeated total internal reflections at the boundary, allowing it to travel long distances with minimal loss.

questions.question: Derive the lens formula for a thin convex lens using ray diagrams and similar triangles.
questions.answer: Considering rays parallel to the principal axis and passing through the optical centre, similar triangles are formed between object, image and focus points. From geometric relations the lens formula is obtained as 1/f = 1/v − 1/u.

questions.question: A convex lens of focal length 20 cm forms an image of an object placed 30 cm away. Calculate the position and nature of the image.
questions.answer: Using lens formula: 1/f = 1/v − 1/u. Here f = 20 cm, u = -30 cm. 1/20 = 1/v + 1/30. So 1/v = 1/20 − 1/30 = 1/60. Therefore v = 60 cm. The image is real and inverted.

questions.question: Explain image formation by a convex lens for different object positions with ray diagrams.
questions.answer: Beyond 2F → image between F and 2F (real, inverted). At 2F → image at 2F (same size). Between F and 2F → image beyond 2F (enlarged). At F → image at infinity. Between F and lens → virtual, erect, enlarged image.

questions.question: Explain why a concave lens always forms a virtual, erect and diminished image.
questions.answer: A concave lens diverges parallel light rays. The refracted rays appear to originate from a point on the same side of the lens as the object, forming a virtual, erect and smaller image.

questions.question: Derive the expression for magnification produced by a lens.
questions.answer: Magnification is defined as m = height of image / height of object. Using geometry of lens ray diagrams, m = v/u.

questions.question: A concave lens of focal length 15 cm forms an image 10 cm from the lens. Find the position of the object.
questions.answer: Using lens formula: 1/f = 1/v − 1/u. Here f = -15 cm, v = -10 cm. -1/15 = -1/10 − 1/u. Solving gives u = -30 cm.

questions.question: Explain the phenomenon of dispersion of white light through a glass prism.
questions.answer: When white light passes through a prism, it splits into its constituent colours forming a spectrum. This occurs because different wavelengths of light refract by different amounts.

questions.question: Explain the formation of a rainbow in the sky.
questions.answer: A rainbow forms when sunlight enters raindrops and undergoes refraction, dispersion, total internal reflection and refraction again. This separates the light into its component colours forming a circular arc in the sky.

questions.question: Explain atmospheric refraction and describe how it causes advanced sunrise and delayed sunset.
questions.answer: Due to atmospheric refraction, sunlight bends as it passes through layers of atmosphere with varying densities. This makes the Sun appear slightly above its actual position before sunrise and after sunset, causing earlier sunrise and later sunset.

questions.question: Explain why the Sun appears flattened at the horizon during sunrise or sunset.
questions.answer: During sunrise and sunset, light from the lower part of the Sun is refracted more than light from the upper part due to atmospheric refraction. This unequal bending makes the Sun appear flattened.
answer_outline: Different refraction of upper and lower rays

questions.question: Explain the working of a simple microscope and derive its magnification formula.
questions.answer: A simple microscope uses a convex lens. When the object is placed within the focal length, a virtual enlarged image is formed. The magnification for normal adjustment is given by M = 1 + D/f.

questions.question: Describe an experiment to determine the focal length of a convex lens using the distant object method.
questions.answer: A convex lens is placed facing a distant object. A screen is moved until a sharp image forms. The distance between the lens and screen gives the focal length.

questions.question: Describe an experiment to trace the path of a ray of light through a glass slab.
questions.answer: A rectangular glass slab is placed on paper and its outline traced. Pins are used to mark incident and emergent rays. Angles of incidence and refraction are measured to verify Snell's law.

questions.question: Explain the structure and functioning of the human eye with a labelled description.
questions.answer: The human eye consists of cornea, iris, pupil, lens and retina. Light enters through the cornea, passes through the pupil and lens, and forms an image on the retina which is transmitted to the brain via the optic nerve.

questions.question: Explain the defect of myopia and how it is corrected.
questions.answer: Myopia is a defect where distant objects appear blurred because the image forms in front of the retina. It is corrected using a concave lens which diverges light rays so the image forms on the retina.
answer_outline: Reason image forms in front of retina

questions.question: Explain the defect of hypermetropia and its correction.
questions.answer: Hypermetropia is a defect where nearby objects cannot be seen clearly because the image forms behind the retina. It is corrected using a convex lens which converges light rays so the image forms on the retina.
